meta.name: AUDIT-C Alcohol Use Brief Screen
meta.fullName: Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test - Consumption
meta.description: The AUDIT-C is a brief 3-item alcohol screening tool derived from the first 3 questions of the full AUDIT. It identifies hazardous drinking or active alcohol use disorders, and is widely used in primary care settings.
meta.author: Bush et al. (1998)
meta.reference: Bush K, Kivlahan DR, McDonell MB, Fihn SD, Bradley KA. The AUDIT alcohol consumption questions (AUDIT-C): an effective brief screening test for problem drinking. Arch Intern Med. 1998;158(16):1789-1795.
meta.instruction: Please answer the following questions about your alcohol use. Select the option that best describes your drinking habits.

questions.text: How often do you have a drink containing alcohol?
How many standard drinks containing alcohol do you have on a typical day when you are drinking?
How often do you have 6 or more drinks on one occasion?

ranges.label: Low Risk
ranges.description: Your alcohol consumption is within a low-risk range. No significant concerns about drinking habits detected.
suggestions: Continue maintaining moderate or no drinking habits
Be mindful of situations where alcohol use may increase
Stay informed about recommended drinking limits

ranges.label: Hazardous Drinking
ranges.description: Your drinking pattern suggests hazardous alcohol use that may increase risk of health problems.
suggestions: Consider reducing the amount and frequency of your drinking
Track your alcohol consumption to become more aware of your habits
Set personal limits before you start drinking
Consider speaking with a healthcare provider about your drinking habits

ranges.label: Harmful or Dependent Drinking
ranges.description: Your drinking pattern suggests harmful alcohol use or possible dependence. This level of consumption carries significant health risks.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to consult a healthcare provider about your alcohol use
Consider seeking specialized help for alcohol-related concerns
Explore resources such as support groups or counseling
Avoid driving or operating machinery after drinking
Talk to someone you trust about your drinking
